statement: Food and non-alcoholic beverage prices in Iceland are approximately 50–70% higher than the EU-27 average, according to Eurostat comparative price level indices (2024). This makes Iceland one of the most expensive countries in Europe for food.
caveats: High food prices reflect multiple factors: transport costs, small market, agricultural protection, import tariffs, and VAT. EU membership alone would not eliminate all price differences — Norway, also outside the EU's CAP, has similarly high food prices.

statement: Iceland applies high import tariffs on many agricultural products: up to 80% ad valorem on dairy products (effective tariff-rate equivalents can exceed 100% when specific duties per kg are included), 55% on meat, and 30–55% on various processed foods. These tariffs protect domestic agriculture but contribute to higher consumer prices. Under EU membership, these tariffs would be replaced by the EU's Common External Tariff and internal free trade, significantly restructuring the sector.
caveats: Agricultural tariffs are politically sensitive. The farming sector employs a small share of the workforce (~3%) but has significant cultural and political importance, particularly in rural areas.
statement_is: Ísland leggur háa innflutningstolla á margar landbúnaðarafurðir: allt að 80% verðtoll á mjólkurvörur (raunverulegt tollígildi getur farið yfir 100% þegar kilógrammatollur er reiknaður inn), 55% á kjöt og 30–55% á ýmsan unninn mat. Tollarnir vernda innlendan landbúnað en stuðla að hærra matvælaverði. Með ESB-aðild myndu þessir tollar víkja fyrir sameiginlegum ytri tollum ESB og innri fríverslun.
caveats_is: Tollar á landbúnaðarvörur eru pólitískt viðkvæmt mál. Landbúnaðurinn hefur fáa í vinnu, eða um 3% vinnuaflsins, en hefur verulega menningarlega og pólitíska þýðingu, sérstaklega í dreifbýli.

statement: The EU Common Agricultural Policy (CAP) provides direct payments to farmers, rural development support, and market intervention measures. The CAP budget for 2021–2027 is approximately €387 billion, making it the largest single item in the EU budget (~31%). Iceland's agricultural sector would be eligible for CAP support upon EU membership.
source_name: European Commission — CAP at a glance
caveats: The net effect of CAP on Icelandic farmers would depend on how current Icelandic subsidies compare to CAP payments. Icelandic agriculture is heavily subsidised domestically, so the transition could be complex.

statement: The European Union is Iceland's largest trading partner. In 2024, approximately 50% of Iceland's goods exports and 60% of goods imports by value were with EU member states. Marine products (primarily fish) constitute the largest single export category to the EU.
caveats: Trade shares fluctuate with commodity prices and exchange rates. The UK's departure from the EU in 2020 shifted some trade flows.

statement: Through the EEA Agreement, Iceland already has tariff-free access to the EU single market for most goods (excluding agricultural and fishery products). EU membership would extend this to full customs union membership, eliminating remaining tariffs and customs procedures but requiring Iceland to adopt the EU's common external tariff.
source_name: EEA Agreement — Protocol 3 on trade in processed agricultural products
caveats: The EEA already provides extensive market access. The incremental trade benefit of full EU membership would primarily come from agricultural/fisheries trade liberalisation and elimination of rules-of-origin requirements.

statement: Iceland currently contributes to the EEA/EFTA Grants mechanism, which provides approximately €180 million per year (shared among Iceland, Norway, and Liechtenstein) to reduce social and economic disparities in the EEA. Norway pays the vast majority. As an EU member, Iceland's estimated net budget contribution would be significantly higher, though the exact amount depends on GDP and negotiated rebates.
caveats: Estimating Iceland's net EU budget contribution is complex. As a high-GDP-per-capita country, Iceland would be a net contributor, but the exact amount depends on CAP receipts, structural funds, and other transfers.
